question_text: Joints that allow no movement are called:
explanation: Fibrous joints (synarthroses) are immovable joints where bones are held together by dense fibrous connective tissue with no joint cavity. Examples include sutures of the skull where the flat bones of the cranium are tightly interlocked. These joints provide structural stability and protection.

elimination_hints.hint: Cartilaginous joints (amphiarthroses) allow limited movement, not zero movement
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing slightly movable joints with immovable joints

elimination_hints.hint: Synovial joints (diarthroses) are freely movable joints — the opposite of immovable
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing freely movable with immovable joints

elimination_hints.hint: Ball-and-socket is a type of synovial joint allowing maximum movement (e.g., shoulder, hip)
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the most mobile joint type with immovable joints

question_text: The joint between the skull bones (sutures) is an example of:
explanation: Skull sutures are fibrous joints where the edges of cranial bones are connected by dense fibrous connective tissue (sutural ligaments). These joints are immovable in adults and provide a tight, protective enclosure for the brain. In newborns, the sutures are wider and have fontanelles (soft spots) to allow brain growth.

elimination_hints.hint: Synovial joints have a joint cavity with synovial fluid — skull sutures have no cavity
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing immovable fibrous joints with freely movable synovial joints

elimination_hints.hint: Cartilaginous joints have cartilage between bones (e.g., intervertebral discs) — sutures have fibrous tissue
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the connecting material in skull joints

elimination_hints.hint: Pivot joints allow rotation (e.g., atlas-axis) — skull sutures allow no movement
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a specific synovial joint type with a fibrous joint

question_text: The shoulder joint is an example of which type of synovial joint?
explanation: The shoulder (glenohumeral) joint is a ball-and-socket joint where the rounded head of the humerus fits into the shallow glenoid cavity of the scapula. Ball-and-socket joints allow the greatest range of movement — flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, rotation, and circumduction. The hip joint is also a ball-and-socket joint.

elimination_hints.hint: Hinge joints allow movement in one plane only (like a door hinge) — the shoulder allows multi-directional movement
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing limited uniaxial movement with multiaxial movement

elimination_hints.hint: Pivot joints allow rotation around an axis (e.g., atlas-axis) — the shoulder allows much more than rotation
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing rotational-only joints with multiaxial joints

elimination_hints.hint: Gliding joints allow sliding movements between flat surfaces (e.g., between carpals) — the shoulder has a ball-in-socket configuration
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing flat sliding joints with ball-and-socket joints

question_text: Synovial fluid is secreted by:
explanation: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane (synovium), which lines the inner surface of the joint capsule in synovial joints. This viscous fluid lubricates the joint surfaces, reduces friction during movement, provides nutrients to articular cartilage (which is avascular), and acts as a shock absorber.

elimination_hints.hint: Periosteum is the outer covering of bone — it doesn't line joint cavities or secrete synovial fluid
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing bone covering with joint lining

elimination_hints.hint: Articular cartilage covers bone ends in joints — it receives nutrition from synovial fluid but doesn't secrete it
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the cartilage lining with the secretory membrane

elimination_hints.hint: Ligaments connect bone to bone and stabilize joints — they don't secrete fluid
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing structural joint components with secretory structures

question_text: The elbow joint is an example of:
explanation: The elbow joint is a hinge joint (ginglymus) that allows movement in only one plane — flexion (bending) and extension (straightening). Like a door hinge, it permits angular movement in a single axis. The knee joint is also primarily a hinge joint.

elimination_hints.hint: Ball-and-socket joints allow multiaxial movement — the elbow only allows flexion and extension
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the range of motion at the elbow

elimination_hints.hint: Saddle joints allow biaxial movement — the classic example is the thumb (carpometacarpal joint)
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the elbow with the thumb joint type

elimination_hints.hint: Condyloid (ellipsoid) joints allow biaxial movement — found at the wrist (radiocarpal joint)
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the elbow with the wrist joint type

question_text: The pivot joint between the atlas and axis vertebrae allows:
explanation: The pivot joint (trochoid joint) between the atlas (C1) and axis (C2) allows rotational movement of the head — the 'no' movement (turning head side to side). The odontoid process (dens) of the axis projects upward and acts as a pivot around which the atlas rotates, carrying the skull with it.
correct_answer: Rotational movement of the head (turning side to side)

options.text: Nodding movement of the head (up and down)
Rotational movement of the head (turning side to side)

elimination_hints.hint: Nodding (yes movement) occurs at the atlanto-occipital joint (atlas-skull), not atlas-axis
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the atlanto-occipital joint with the atlanto-axial joint

elimination_hints.hint: Lateral bending involves multiple cervical vertebrae, not specifically the atlas-axis pivot joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the specific pivot movement with general cervical flexibility

elimination_hints.hint: Circumduction occurs at ball-and-socket joints (shoulder, hip), not at pivot joints
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiaxial movement with rotational-only movement

question_text: Cartilaginous joints with slight movement are found between:
explanation: Cartilaginous joints (amphiarthroses) are joints where bones are connected by cartilage, allowing limited movement. The intervertebral discs between adjacent vertebrae are the classic example — they consist of fibrocartilage that allows slight bending and twisting. The pubic symphysis is another example of a cartilaginous joint.

elimination_hints.hint: Skull sutures are fibrous joints (immovable), not cartilaginous joints
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fibrous joints with cartilaginous joints

elimination_hints.hint: The shoulder joint is a synovial joint (freely movable), not a cartilaginous joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing freely movable synovial joints with slightly movable cartilaginous joints

elimination_hints.hint: The knee joint (femur-tibia) is a synovial hinge joint, not a cartilaginous joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a major synovial joint with a cartilaginous joint

question_text: Which of the following is a saddle joint in the human body?
explanation: The carpometacarpal (CMC) joint of the thumb is the classic example of a saddle joint. In this joint, the concave and convex surfaces of the trapezium bone and the first metacarpal fit together like a rider on a saddle. This biaxial joint allows flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and opposition — giving the thumb its remarkable dexterity.

elimination_hints.hint: The knee is primarily a hinge joint (with some rotation), not a saddle joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hinge joints with saddle joints

elimination_hints.hint: The elbow is a hinge joint allowing flexion-extension only
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between different synovial joint types

elimination_hints.hint: The hip is a ball-and-socket joint, not a saddle joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ball-and-socket with saddle joint

question_text: Ligaments connect:
explanation: Ligaments are bands of dense regular connective tissue that connect bone to bone. They stabilize joints and limit excessive movement. For example, the anterior and posterior cruciate ligaments (ACL, PCL) in the knee connect the femur to the tibia. Tendons, in contrast, connect muscle to bone.

elimination_hints.hint: Muscle to bone connections are made by tendons, not ligaments
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ligaments with tendons

elimination_hints.hint: There is no standard connective tissue specifically connecting muscle to muscle
elimination_hints.misconception: Imagining a non-existent anatomical connection

elimination_hints.hint: Cartilage-to-bone connections are part of bone development, not ligament function
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing skeletal development with joint stabilization

question_text: Gliding joints (plane joints) allow:
explanation: Gliding joints (plane/arthrodial joints) have flat or slightly curved articular surfaces that allow sliding or gliding movements in multiple directions. They permit limited translational movement without angular or rotational motion. Examples include joints between carpal bones (intercarpal joints), tarsal bones (intertarsal joints), and between the sternum and clavicle.
correct_answer: Sliding or gliding movements between flat bone surfaces

options.text: Sliding or gliding movements between flat bone surfaces
Movement in all directions including circumduction

elimination_hints.hint: Rotation around a single axis is characteristic of pivot joints, not gliding joints
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pivot joint movement with gliding joint movement

elimination_hints.hint: Flexion and extension in one plane describes hinge joints, not gliding joints
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hinge movement with gliding movement

elimination_hints.hint: Circumduction is characteristic of ball-and-socket joints — gliding joints have much more limited movement
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the range of motion in gliding joints

question_text: Gouty arthritis is caused by the deposition of crystals of:
explanation: Gout (gouty arthritis) is caused by the deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joint spaces, particularly in the metatarsophalangeal joint of the big toe. These crystals form when blood uric acid levels are elevated (hyperuricemia), often due to purine-rich diet, impaired renal excretion, or metabolic disorders. The crystal deposition triggers an intense inflammatory response.

elimination_hints.hint: Calcium oxalate crystals form kidney stones, not joint deposits
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing kidney stone composition with joint crystal deposition

elimination_hints.hint: Calcium phosphate is a component of bone (hydroxyapatite), not the cause of gout
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing bone mineral with gout-causing crystals

elimination_hints.hint: Cholesterol deposits form atherosclerotic plaques in blood vessels, not joint crystals
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing vascular disease with joint disease

question_text: Rheumatoid arthritis differs from osteoarthritis in that it is:
explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease where the body's immune system mistakenly attacks the synovial membrane of joints, causing inflammation, swelling, pain, and eventually joint destruction. It typically affects both sides symmetrically. Osteoarthritis (OA) is a degenerative (wear-and-tear) disease affecting articular cartilage, usually asymmetric, and is not autoimmune in nature.
correct_answer: An autoimmune disease attacking the synovial membrane

options.text: Caused by wear and tear of articular cartilage
An autoimmune disease attacking the synovial membrane
Caused by uric acid crystal deposition

elimination_hints.hint: Wear and tear of cartilage describes osteoarthritis — the question asks how RA differs from OA
elimination_hints.misconception: Describing osteoarthritis instead of rheumatoid arthritis

elimination_hints.hint: Uric acid crystal deposition causes gout, not rheumatoid arthritis
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing gout with rheumatoid arthritis

elimination_hints.hint: RA can affect any joint and typically affects small joints of hands and feet symmetrically
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying osteoarthritis pattern (weight-bearing joints) to rheumatoid arthritis

question_text:
Assertion (A): Synovial joints are the most movable type of joints in the body.
Reason (R): Synovial joints have a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid that lubricates the articulating surfaces.
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Synovial joints (diarthroses) are the most freely movable joints because they have a joint cavity containing synovial fluid that reduces friction between the smooth articular cartilage surfaces. This lubrication allows free, pain-free movement in various directions depending on the joint type.
correct_answer: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The synovial fluid lubrication directly enables the free movement — it's the correct explanation
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing how lubrication enables mobility

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is anatomically accurate — synovial joints do have fluid-filled cavities
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the structure of synovial joints

elimination_hints.hint: Synovial joints are indeed the most movable joint type — this is a fundamental fact
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the mobility classification of joints

question_text:
Assertion (A): Tendons and ligaments are both made of dense regular connective tissue.
Reason (R): Tendons connect muscles to bones while ligaments connect bones to bones.
explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, but the reason is NOT the correct explanation of the assertion. The assertion states that both are made of dense regular connective tissue (primarily parallel collagen fibres), which is true. The reason correctly describes their different functions (tendons: muscle-to-bone; ligaments: bone-to-bone). However, the functional difference doesn't explain why both are composed of the same tissue type — that's because both structures need high tensile strength in one direction.
correct_answer: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason describes what tendons and ligaments connect — this doesn't explain why they're made of the same tissue
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking functional description explains structural composition

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct — tendons do connect muscle to bone and ligaments connect bone to bone
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting basic anatomical connections

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true — both tendons and ligaments are made of dense regular connective tissue
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the tissue type of tendons and ligaments

question_text:
Assertion (A): The knee joint is primarily a hinge joint but also allows some rotational movement.
Reason (R): The knee joint has cruciate ligaments (ACL and PCL) that allow rotation when the knee is flexed.
explanation: The assertion is true — the knee is primarily a hinge joint (flexion/extension) but does allow some rotation when flexed. However, the reason is false — the cruciate ligaments (ACL and PCL) actually restrict excessive rotation and anterior-posterior displacement. The slight rotation is permitted by the joint's anatomical design (curved condyles and menisci), not by the cruciate ligaments. The cruciate ligaments stabilize and limit movement rather than enable rotation.
correct_answer: A is true but R is false

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Cruciate ligaments restrict rotation — they don't enable it
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking stabilizing ligaments promote movement rather than limit it

elimination_hints.hint: The reason states that cruciate ligaments 'allow rotation' — this is incorrect; they restrict it
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the reason as true when it's factually wrong about ligament function

elimination_hints.hint: The knee joint does allow some rotation when flexed — this is well-established in joint anatomy
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the minor rotational capability of the knee

question_text:
Match the following joint types with their examples:

Column I:
(i) Ball-and-socket joint
(ii) Hinge joint
(iii) Pivot joint
(iv) Gliding joint

Column II:
(P) Between carpal bones
(Q) Shoulder joint
(R) Elbow joint
(S) Atlas-axis joint
explanation: Ball-and-socket joint (i-Q) — the shoulder joint has the humerus head fitting into the glenoid cavity. Hinge joint (ii-R) — the elbow allows flexion/extension in one plane. Pivot joint (iii-S) — the atlas-axis joint allows rotation of the head. Gliding joint (iv-P) — intercarpal joints allow sliding movements between the small flat bones of the wrist.

elimination_hints.hint: The elbow is a hinge joint (not ball-and-socket) — it only allows flexion/extension
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the elbow with a ball-and-socket joint

elimination_hints.hint: The atlas-axis is a pivot joint for rotation, not a ball-and-socket joint
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing rotational pivot with multiaxial ball-and-socket

elimination_hints.hint: Carpal bones have flat gliding joints, not a ball-and-socket arrangement
elimination_hints.misconception: Mismatching all joint types with their examples

question_text:
Match the following joint disorders with their primary characteristics:

Column I:
(i) Osteoarthritis
(ii) Rheumatoid arthritis
(iii) Gout
(iv) Osteoporosis

Column II:
(P) Autoimmune attack on synovial membrane
(Q) Uric acid crystal deposition in joints
(R) Decreased bone density and increased fracture risk
(S) Degeneration of articular cartilage due to wear and tear
explanation: Osteoarthritis (i-S) is degenerative wear-and-tear of articular cartilage in weight-bearing joints. Rheumatoid arthritis (ii-P) is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks synovial membranes. Gout (iii-Q) results from monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints due to hyperuricemia. Osteoporosis (iv-R) is decreased bone mineral density leading to fragile bones, though it's technically a bone disease rather than a joint disorder.

elimination_hints.hint: Osteoarthritis is degenerative (wear-and-tear), not autoimmune — RA is the autoimmune one
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the mechanisms of OA and RA

elimination_hints.hint: Osteoarthritis involves cartilage degeneration, not uric acid — gout involves uric acid
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing degenerative cartilage disease with crystal arthropathy

elimination_hints.hint: Osteoarthritis doesn't involve decreased bone density — that's osteoporosis
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing osteoarthritis with osteoporosis

question_text: Statement: Tendons connect muscle to bone, while ligaments connect bone to bone.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Tendons are tough, inelastic bands of dense regular connective tissue that attach skeletal muscles to bones, transmitting the force of muscle contraction to produce movement. Ligaments are slightly more elastic bands of dense regular connective tissue that connect adjacent bones at joints, providing stability and limiting excessive movement.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a fundamental anatomical distinction — tendons to bone, ligaments bone-to-bone
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the roles of tendons and ligaments

question_text: The joints between the _______ bones of the wrist are examples of gliding (plane) joints.
explanation: The carpal bones of the wrist form gliding (plane) joints with each other. There are 8 carpal bones arranged in two rows that articulate with each other through flat or slightly curved surfaces. These intercarpal joints allow small sliding and gliding movements that collectively provide wrist flexibility.

elimination_hints.hint: Tarsal bones are in the ankle/foot, not the wrist — though intertarsal joints are also gliding
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing wrist bones (carpals) with ankle bones (tarsals)

elimination_hints.hint: Metacarpals are the palm bones — the question specifically asks about wrist bones
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing palm bones with wrist bones

elimination_hints.hint: Phalanges are finger bones that form hinge joints, not gliding joints
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing finger bones with wrist bones

question_text: A 45-year-old man presents with sudden severe pain, redness, and swelling of the big toe joint after a heavy protein-rich meal. Blood tests reveal elevated serum uric acid levels. Which condition is he most likely suffering from?
explanation: The classic presentation of acute onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in the first metatarsophalangeal (big toe) joint, triggered by a protein-rich (purine-rich) meal, with elevated serum uric acid, is diagnostic of gout (gouty arthritis). Purines from the diet are metabolized to uric acid. When levels exceed the saturation point, monosodium urate crystals precipitate in the joint, triggering intense inflammation.

elimination_hints.hint: RA is chronic, symmetric, and autoimmune — it doesn't present as sudden monoarticular attack after a meal
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing acute crystal arthritis with chronic autoimmune arthritis

elimination_hints.hint: OA is gradual-onset degenerative disease — it doesn't cause sudden acute inflammation triggered by diet
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing acute inflammatory presentation with chronic degenerative disease

elimination_hints.hint: Osteoporosis affects bone density and causes fractures — it doesn't cause acute joint inflammation
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a bone density disorder with an inflammatory joint condition
